title: Algebraic Torsion of Concave Boundaries of Linear Plumbings
speaker: Joanna Nelson
abstract: Algebraic torsion is a means of understanding the topological complexity of certain homomorphic curves counted in some Floer theories of contact manifolds.  This talk focuses on algebraic torsion and the contact invariant in embedded contact homology, which has largely been left unexplored over the past decade, but can be used to understand symplectic fillability and overtwistedness of the contact 3-manifold.  We restrict to concave linear plumbings, whose boundaries are contact lens spaces, and explain the curve counting methods we have developed and results that we have obtained, which parallels with the contact toric description of these lens spaces. This talk is based on joint work in progress with Aleksandra Marinkovic, Ana Rechtman, Laura Starkston, Shira Tanny, and Luya Wang.  We are exploring the use of our methods to better understand nonfillable tight contact 3-manifolds obtained from more general plumbings.

proof_stages.label: Motivating fillability and ECH-computation problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the project as seeking tight but nonfillable contact manifolds and, in the nearer term, developing ECH and algebraic-torsion tools for contact manifolds more topologically interesting than the standard computed cases.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic linear plumbings and concave boundaries
proof_stages.summary: Linear plumbings are defined from graph-labeled disc bundles, given symplectic neighborhood models and boundary contact forms, and characterized by linear-algebra criteria for convexity or concavity, with the relevant linear cases producing lens spaces.

proof_stages.label: Global toric model and explicit Reeb dynamics
proof_stages.summary: Starting from Gay-Stipsicz local toric models, the collaborators assemble a global toric description of a linear plumbing, making the moment cone, the Morse-Bott Reeb tori, and the contact forms explicit enough for both toric and holomorphic-curve computations.

proof_stages.label: Toric classification proof of tightness versus overtwistedness
proof_stages.summary: Using Lerman's classification of toric contact 3-manifolds by two defining rays and their angle, the global toric plumbing model gives a direct criterion: angle at most pi yields the canonical tight lens-space contact structure, while angle greater than pi yields an overtwisted one.

proof_stages.label: ECH and contact-invariant framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews nondegenerate Reeb orbits, ECH generators, the ECH index and index inequality, the differential by low-index currents, Taubes's isomorphism with monopole Floer homology, and the resulting contact invariant facts needed for the curve-side proof.

proof_stages.label: J-plus filtration and algebraic torsion
proof_stages.summary: Algebraic torsion is described as the page of a J_+-filtered spectral sequence on which the empty set dies, with caveats that ECH algebraic torsion is not known to be contact-form invariant and with a simple-subcomplex version giving a weak cobordism monotonicity bound.

proof_stages.label: Holomorphic-plane calculation in the toric setting
proof_stages.summary: The holomorphic-curve approach states the almost-theorem in moment-cone terms and reduces the contact-invariant and algebraic-torsion conclusions to constructing Fredholm/ECH-index-one planes in the contact toric model and proving the required uniqueness or nonuniqueness, with a borderline angle-pi case left less sharp than the toric method.

examples.name: Macaroni-noodle holomorphic curves
examples.note: The two-positive-ended cylinder nicknamed a macaroni noodle is used as a low-complexity curve type illustrating algebraic torsion order one, not as a load-bearing construction in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses macaroni-noodle curves concretely in the algebraic-torsion spectral sequence, explaining that their two positive ends kill the empty set on the second page and give torsion order one
